title: Needle-felted toad
subtitle: A garden toad in mottled brown and olive merino
excerpt: Build a squat, wide-bodied toad from mottled brown and olive merino using needle-felting. The toad is posed in a sitting position with broad flat feet and prominent golden eyes, and works well as part of a garden wildlife display.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting workshop practice. Toad and frog forms are a common beginner project in garden-creature felting series.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry sculpting method using barbed needles. Repeated stabs drive the barbs through the wool fibres, catching and tangling them into a dense, shaped mass without any water or heat.

content.text: A toad is broader and more squat than a frog. The body is nearly spherical, the head is wide and blunt, and the hind legs fold out at the sides rather than tucking under the body. This version does not use wire legs: the legs are small flat pads of merino attached to the underside, giving the impression of a resting toad without the complexity of wire.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Merino roving, olive or khaki green (mottling)
Merino roving, pale cream or yellow (underside)

content.text: Fold 10 g of brown merino into a wide, slightly flattened oval about 6 cm long and 5 cm wide. A toad body is notably wider relative to its length than most animals. Needle all over with the size-36 needle to compress and firm the core, rotating constantly. The finished core should be about 3 cm tall.

Roll 3 g of brown merino into a wide, blunt oval about 3 cm wide. Needle to firmness. Position it at the front of the body, slightly raised so the head appears to sit up from the body surface. Blend the join with bridging wisps of merino.

Pull small wisps of olive merino and dot them irregularly across the upper body and head surface. Needle each patch in with the size-40 needle using short, light strokes. The patches should sit on the surface and blend softly at the edges; they should not disappear into the brown entirely. Add a pale cream patch to the underside.
Step 4: Build and attach the legs

For the front feet, needle two small flat pads of brown merino about 1.5 cm long. Attach them to the underside at the front corners. For the hind legs, needle two larger, slightly curved pads about 3 cm long. Attach them to the sides of the rear body, folded out at right angles so the toad appears to crouch.

Toad eyes are round and protrude from the top of the head. Roll two small spheres of amber merino about 5 mm across. Needle each one to a firm ball, then position them on the upper surface of the head, one on each side toward the back. Needle the base of each eye sphere firmly into the head. Add a thin horizontal line of black merino across each amber eye for the distinctive toad pupil shape.
Finish the whole surface with the size-40 needle to smooth any rough areas. The completed
work should look consistent with no visible joins or unfelted patches.

items.problem: The olive mottling looks blotchy rather than natural
items.cause: The wisps were too large and the edges too abrupt
items.fix: Use much smaller wisps of olive, torn into fragments about 1 cm across. Needle them lightly so they blend softly into the background. Layer a second application of smaller wisps over the first once the first layer is needled in.

items.problem: The eyes pop off after handling
items.cause: The base of the eye ball was not needled deeply enough into the head surface
items.fix: Hold the eye ball in position and needle repeatedly through the base into the head. Add a thin annular ring of merino around the base of each eye ball and needle it flat to lock the join.
